---
title: "Real Income vs Housing Prices in Singapore"
subtitle: "AAI1001 – Data Engineering and Visualization | AY25/26 Tri 2"
author:
  - name: "<span style='font-size: 0.55em; font-weight: normal; line-height: 1.8;'>Shina Shih Xin Rong (2402781)<br>Jeanie Cherie Chua Yue-Ning (2403083)<br>Claudia Yue Xin Ying (2401817)<br>Nurul Zahirah Binte Muhamadnoh (2401671)<br>Anushka Chourasia (2503769)</span>"
date: 2026-03-24
date-format: "dddd MMM DD, YYYY"

toc: true
toc-depth: 1
number-sections: false
engine: knitr
format:
  revealjs:
    self-contained: true
    embed-resources: true
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    margin: 0.05
    min-scale: 0.2
    max-scale: 2.0
    scrollable: true
    slide-number: true
    preview-links: false
    incremental: false
    footer: "AAI1001 Team Project – Real Income vs Housing Prices in Singapore"
    html-table-processing: none
    code-block-height: 700px
---

# 1.0 Original Visualisation & Critique
```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(gt)
library(scales)

tidy_data <- readRDS("tidy_data.rds")

gap_data <- tidy_data |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = Measure_Type, values_from = Index_Value) |>
  mutate(Affordability_Gap = `HDB Resale Price` - `Real Income`)

col_measure <- c("Real Income" = "#2166AC", "HDB Resale Price" = "#B2182B")
col_group   <- c("Bottom 20%" = "#D73027", "Middle 60%" = "#FC8D59",
                  "Top 20%" = "#4575B4")

base_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 20, margin = margin(b = 6)),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 13, color = "grey40", margin = margin(b = 12)),
    plot.caption     = element_text(size = 9, color = "grey55", hjust = 0, margin = margin(t = 10)),
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "#FAFAFA", color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#FAFAFA", color = NA),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey90"),
    legend.position  = "bottom",
    legend.key.width = unit(1.5, "cm"),
    legend.text      = element_text(size = 12),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14, margin = margin(b = 6)),
    axis.text        = element_text(color = "grey30"),
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 11)
  )

events <- tibble(
  xmin  = c(2008, 2020),
  xmax  = c(2009, 2021),
  label = c("GFC", "COVID-19")
)
```
```{css, echo=FALSE}
code.sourceCode { font-size: 1.2em; }
.reveal .cell-output-stdout { font-size: 1.2em; }
```

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Shina.** Welcome everyone. Today our team will present our analysis of whether real income growth in Singapore kept pace with HDB resale price growth between 2000 and 2025. We begin with the original visualisation that inspired this project, then show how we redesigned it using clearer data choices, a transparent data pipeline, and a more honest visual comparison.
:::


## 1.1 Previous Visualisation {style="font-size: 0.80em;"}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

::: {style="background: #2166AC; color: white; padding: 12px 18px; border-radius: 6px; font-size: 0.85em; margin-bottom: 16px;"}
**Research Question:** *Did real income growth for lower-income households keep pace with housing price growth between 2000 and 2025, and have affordability gaps widened across income groups over time?*
:::

- **~80% of Singapore residents** live in HDB flats
- Housing is the **single largest financial commitment** most families make
- When prices outpace income → **longer saving horizons, higher debt burdens**
- Direct policy relevance for **housing grants, CPF policy, income support**

:::
::: {.column width="45%"}

::: {style="text-align: center; font-weight: bold; font-size: 0.75em; color: #555; margin-bottom: 6px;"}
Previous Visualisation
:::

![](Average-Household-Income-Per-Household-Image.jpg){width=100%}

::: {style="font-size: 0.6em; color: grey; margin-top: 4px;"}
Source: Stacked — [*Avg Household Income vs PPI*](https://stackedhomes.com/has-our-household-income-really-overtaken-growth-in-property-prices/#sh.zta0kv), 2010–2020.
:::

:::
::::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Shina.** We chose this visualisation from the property blog Stacked, which plots average household income by decile against the Private Property Index. It caught our attention because housing affordability is a pressing issue in Singapore, where about 80% of residents live in HDB flats. Our research question asks whether real income growth — especially for lower-income households — has kept pace with housing price growth over the past 25 years.
:::


## 1.2 Strengths of the Original {style="font-size: 0.72em;"}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {style="text-align: center; font-weight: bold; font-size: 0.7em; color: #555; margin-bottom: 4px;"}
Previous Visualisation
:::

![](Average-Household-Income-Per-Household-Image.jpg){width=95%}

::: {style="font-size: 0.6em; color: grey; margin-top: 4px;"}
Source: Stacked — [*Avg Household Income vs PPI*](https://stackedhomes.com/has-our-household-income-really-overtaken-growth-in-property-prices/#sh.zta0kv), 2010–2020.
:::

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**What the original does well:**

- Covers a **meaningful time span** (2010–2020) with consistent annual data
- Uses **official decile-level income data** — granular and authoritative
- Poses a **relevant question** that resonates with Singapore's housing discourse
- Clean, **minimal layout** with a clear title and readable axis labels

:::
::::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Shina.** Before we dive into weaknesses, let's acknowledge what the original does well. It uses official SingStat decile data, covers a meaningful time period, and asks a question that resonates with Singaporeans. However, there are several design issues we identified, which I'll walk through on the next slide.
:::


## 1.3 Critique & Proposed Improvements {style="font-size: 0.72em;"}

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false

tribble(
  ~No, ~Weakness, ~Improvement,
  "1", "**Dual y-axis distortion** — falsely suggests correlation", "**Common index (2010 = 100)** — single y-axis",
  "2", "**Ten overlapping lines** — visual congestion", "**Three aggregated groups** — Bottom/Middle/Top",
  "3", "**Nominal income** — overstates real gains", "**Group-specific CPI deflation**",
  "4", "**No housing metric** — general PPI used", "**HDB resale price overlay**",
  "5", "**Dense legend** — high cognitive load", "**Shaded affordability gap ribbon**",
  "6", "**No affordability metric** — must estimate gaps", "**Direct endpoint labels** (2025 values)",
  "7", "**No economic context**", "**GFC & COVID-19 annotations**"
) |>
  gt() |> fmt_markdown(columns = c(Weakness, Improvement)) |>
  cols_label(No = "#", Weakness = "Weakness in Original", Improvement = "Our Improvement") |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = 16, column_labels.background.color = "lightblue",
              column_labels.font.weight = "bold", table.width = pct(100), data_row.padding = px(3)) |>
  cols_width(No ~ px(40), Weakness ~ px(520), Improvement ~ px(520)) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_fill(color = "gray95"), locations = cells_body(rows = c(2, 4, 6))) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = No)
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.72em; margin-top: 10px; color: #555;"}
Each weakness maps to a specific, actionable improvement that we implement in Section 4.
:::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Shina.** Here are seven specific weaknesses we identified. The most critical is the dual y-axis, which distorts the relationship between income and prices. We also address the use of nominal income, the ten congested lines, and the lack of any explicit affordability metric. Each weakness maps directly to an improvement we will demonstrate later. I'll now hand over to Jeanie for the data sources.
:::

# 2.0 Data Selection & Sources


## 2.1 Data Sources {style="font-size: 0.82em;"}

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false

tribble(
  ~Source, ~Dataset, ~Period, ~Purpose,
  "**Department of Statistics Singapore (SingStat)**",
  "Average Monthly Household Employment Income by Decile",
  "2000–2025",
  "Nominal income across 10 deciles, aggregated into 3 groups",

  "**Department of Statistics Singapore (SingStat)**",
  "CPI by Household Income Group (2024 base year)",
  "2000–2025",
  "Group-specific price deflator for real income calculation",

  "**Housing & Development Board / data.gov.sg**",
  "HDB Resale Flat Prices (transaction-level records)",
  "2000–2025",
  "Annual median resale price → housing cost index"
) |>
  gt() |> fmt_markdown(columns = Source) |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = 22, column_labels.background.color = "lightblue",
              column_labels.font.weight = "bold", table.align = "left",
              table.width = pct(100), data_row.padding = px(8)) |>
  cols_width(Source ~ px(380), Dataset ~ px(400), Period ~ px(160), Purpose ~ px(460)) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_fill(color = "gray95"), locations = cells_body(rows = 2))
```
::: {style="font-size: 0.62em; color: grey; line-height: 1.5; margin-top: 10px;"}
**Dataset references**

1. [SingStat — Average Monthly Household Employment Income by Decile](https://tablebuilder.singstat.gov.sg/table/CT/17880)  
2. [SingStat — CPI by Household Income Group](https://www.singstat.gov.sg/whats-new/latest-news/cpi-highlights/)  
3. [data.gov.sg / HDB — Resale Flat Prices](https://data.gov.sg/collections/189/view)
:::
. . .

**Why these sources?**

- SingStat provides the **only official decile-level income breakdown** for Singapore
- Group-specific CPI is available **only from SingStat** (Lowest 20%, Middle 60%, Top 20%)
- HDB resale data from data.gov.sg is the **most granular public record** of actual market prices
- Contextual datasets (Private Property Index, Rental Indices) were explored but not used

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Jeanie.** We used three datasets, all from official Singapore government sources. SingStat gives us two key inputs: household income by decile, and CPI broken down by income group — which is critical for deflating nominal income accurately. The HDB resale flat prices come from data.gov.sg, providing transaction-level records. We explored private property and rental indices but excluded them to keep the scope focused on HDB, which affects the majority of Singaporeans.
:::


## 2.2 Why These Choices Matter {style="font-size: 0.82em;"}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

### 1. Group-specific CPI
- Better reflects how inflation differs across income groups
- Lower-income households spend more on essentials
- Using national CPI would understate affordability pressure

### 2. HDB resale prices
- Most relevant to the majority of Singapore residents
- Captures actual transaction prices
- More appropriate than private housing for our question

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### 3. Annual median
- Robust to extreme transactions
- Avoids distortion from high-value resale outliers

### 4. Base year = 2010
- Allows comparison before and after the base year
- Avoids compressing the series at one end
- Makes indexed interpretation cleaner

:::
::::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Jeanie.** Every data choice has trade-offs, so here we explain why we chose group-specific CPI over national CPI, HDB resale over BTO or private, median over mean, and 2010 as the base year. The most important decision is the group-specific CPI — lower-income households spend a larger share on housing and food, so applying a single national CPI would understate the real cost burden on the Bottom 20%.
:::


## 2.3 Data Flow: Sources to Tidy Dataset {style="font-size: 0.82em;"}

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false

tribble(
  ~Stage, ~Input, ~Output, ~Key_Operation,
  "1. Ingest", "3 raw sources (Excel + CSV)", "3 raw data frames", "read_excel(), read_csv()",
  "2. Clean & Aggregate", "10 deciles × 26 years", "3 groups × 26 years", "case_when() → Bottom 20%, Middle 60%, Top 20%",
  "3. Deflate", "Nominal income + group CPI", "Real income (2024 SGD)", "Real = Nominal ÷ (CPI / 100)",
  "4. Index", "Real income + HDB median", "All series at 2010 = 100", "Index = Value ÷ Value₂₀₁₀ × 100",
  "5. Join & Export", "Income index + HDB index", "tidy_data.rds", "bind_rows() → 156 rows × 4 cols"
) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(Stage = "Stage", Input = "Input", Output = "Output", Key_Operation = "Key Operation") |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = 20, column_labels.background.color = "lightblue",
              column_labels.font.weight = "bold", table.align = "left",
              table.width = pct(100), data_row.padding = px(6)) |>
  cols_width(Stage ~ px(180), Input ~ px(310), Output ~ px(300), Key_Operation ~ px(420)) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_fill(color = "gray95"), locations = cells_body(rows = c(2, 4)))
```

. . .

**Final tidy dataset — 4 analytical dimensions:**

- **Year** — time dimension (2000–2025)
- **Income Group** — distributional dimension (Bottom 20%, Middle 60%, Top 20%)
- **Measure Type** — comparison dimension (Real Income vs HDB Resale Price)
- **Index Value** — quantitative measure (2010 = 100)

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Jeanie.** This table summarises the full pipeline at a glance — from ingesting three raw files to producing a tidy dataset with 156 rows and 4 columns. The key transformation steps are aggregation from 10 deciles into 3 groups, CPI deflation, and rebasing to a common index. Claudia will now walk you through the detailed implementation.
:::

# 3.0 Data Engineering Workflow


## 3.1 Pipeline Overview {style="font-size: 0.75em;"}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

**Pipeline Steps**

```{r}
#| echo: false

tribble(
  ~Step, ~Description,
  "1", "**Import** — Load raw Excel/CSV from SingStat and data.gov.sg",
  "2", "**Clean** — Remove metadata rows, standardize labels, filter 2000–2025",
  "3", "**Aggregate** — Collapse 10 deciles into 3 groups (avg of deciles)",
  "4", "**Deflate** — Apply group-specific CPI: nominal → real income",
  "5", "**Index** — Rebase all series to 2010 = 100",
  "6", "**Join** — Merge income + HDB index by Year × Group",
  "7", "**Validate** — Confirm rebasing, check missing values and row counts",
  "8", "**Export** — Save as tidy_data.rds (156 rows × 4 cols)"
) |>
  gt() |> fmt_markdown(columns = Description) |>
  cols_label(Step = "#", Description = "Pipeline Step") |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = 18, column_labels.background.color = "lightblue",
              column_labels.font.weight = "bold", table.width = pct(100), data_row.padding = px(4)) |>
  cols_width(Step ~ px(40), Description ~ px(700)) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = Step) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_fill(color = "gray95"), locations = cells_body(rows = c(2, 4, 6, 8)))
```

:::
::: {.column width="45%"}

**Key Design Decisions**

```{r}
#| echo: false

tribble(
  ~Decision, ~Rationale,
  "Group-specific CPI over national CPI",
  "Captures different price baskets by income group",
  "Median resale price over mean",
  "Robust to outlier transactions (million-dollar flats)",
  "CPI base year rebasing (2024 → 2010)",
  "Ensures all indices share a common reference year",
  "HDB replicated across groups",
  "Same price index for all groups enables faceted comparison"
) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(Decision = "Decision", Rationale = "Rationale") |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = 17, column_labels.background.color = "lightblue",
              column_labels.font.weight = "bold", table.width = pct(100), data_row.padding = px(4)) |>
  cols_width(Decision ~ px(300), Rationale ~ px(380)) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_fill(color = "gray95"), locations = cells_body(rows = c(2, 4)))
```

:::
::::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Claudia.** This slide summarises the full workflow from raw official datasets to the final analytical dataset used in our charts. The main idea is that our pipeline is not just technical cleaning — it also embeds design decisions that affect interpretation, such as aggregating deciles into three groups, deflating nominal income using group-specific CPI, and rebasing all series to 2010 equals 100. These decisions are what make the final comparison fair and readable.
:::


## 3.2 Code Detail {style="font-size: 0.72em;"}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

**Step 1: Aggregate deciles into income groups**

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

income_grouped <- income_long %>%
  mutate(
    Group = case_when(
      Decile %in% c("1st (Lowest)", "2nd")
        ~ "Bottom 20%",
      Decile %in% c("3rd", "4th", "5th",
                     "6th", "7th", "8th")
        ~ "Middle 60%",
      Decile %in% c("9th", "10th (Highest)")
        ~ "Top 20%"
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(Group, Year) %>%
  summarise(Income_Nominal = mean(Income_Nominal),
            .groups = "drop")
```

**Step 2: Deflate nominal → real income**

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

income_real <- income_grouped %>%
  left_join(cpi_grouped,
            by = c("Group", "Year")) %>%
  mutate(Income_Real = Income_Nominal / (CPI / 100))
```

:::
::: {.column width="45%"}

**Step 3: Rebase to 2010 = 100**

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

base_income <- income_real %>%
  filter(Year == 2010) %>%
  select(Group, Base_Real = Income_Real)

income_indexed <- income_real %>%
  left_join(base_income, by = "Group") %>%
  mutate(Income_Index =
    round(Income_Real / Base_Real * 100, 1))
```

**Step 4: Combine into tidy format**

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

tidy_data <- bind_rows(
  income_long_final,
  hdb_long_final
) %>%
  mutate(
    Income_Group = factor(Income_Group,
      levels = c("Bottom 20%",
                 "Middle 60%", "Top 20%")),
    Measure_Type = factor(Measure_Type,
      levels = c("Real Income",
                 "HDB Resale Price"))
  ) %>%
  arrange(Year, Income_Group, Measure_Type)
```

:::
::::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Claudia.** These four code blocks show the core transformations. Step 1 collapses ten deciles into three meaningful groups using case_when. Step 2 joins group-specific CPI and divides nominal income to get real income. Step 3 rebases everything to 2010 equals 100. And Step 4 binds the income and HDB series into a single tidy data frame. All code is fully reproducible in the data-pipeline.qmd file included in our ZIP submission.
:::


## 3.3 Validation & Quality Checks {style="font-size: 0.78em;"}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Checks Performed

- All indices = **exactly 100 in 2010** ✓
- **No missing values** after joins ✓
- **78 income rows** (26 yrs × 3 groups) ✓
- **156 total rows** (78 income + 78 HDB) ✓
- Cross-checked against raw SingStat tables ✓

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Final Dataset Structure

```{r}
#| echo: false

tribble(
  ~Column, ~Type, ~Description,
  "Year", "integer", "2000–2025",
  "Income_Group", "factor", "Bottom 20%, Middle 60%, Top 20%",
  "Measure_Type", "factor", "Real Income / HDB Resale Price",
  "Index_Value", "numeric", "Index (2010 = 100)"
) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = 22, column_labels.background.color = "lightblue",
              column_labels.font.weight = "bold", data_row.padding = px(6), table.width = pct(100)) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_fill(color = "gray95"), locations = cells_body(rows = c(2, 4)))
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; margin-top: 8px;"}
Full code: `data-pipeline.qmd` (included in ZIP).
:::

:::
::::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Claudia.** Before visualising, we validated the dataset. All indices are exactly 100 in 2010, there are no missing values, and row counts match expectations. The final tidy dataset has just four columns — Year, Income Group, Measure Type, and Index Value — making it straightforward to visualise. Nurul Zahirah will now present the improved visualisation.
:::

# 4.0 Improved Visualisation


## 4.1 Real Income vs HDB Resale Price Index

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 6.5

labels_2025 <- tidy_data |> filter(Year == 2025) |> mutate(label = paste0(round(Index_Value, 0)))

ggplot(tidy_data, aes(x = Year, y = Index_Value, color = Measure_Type, linetype = Measure_Type)) +
  geom_ribbon(data = gap_data, aes(x = Year, ymin = `Real Income`, ymax = `HDB Resale Price`),
              inherit.aes = FALSE, fill = "#B2182B", alpha = 0.07) +
  geom_rect(data = events, aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            inherit.aes = FALSE, fill = "grey50", alpha = 0.10) +
  geom_text(data = events, aes(x = (xmin + xmax) / 2, y = 58, label = label),
            inherit.aes = FALSE, size = 3, color = "grey35", fontface = "italic", angle = 90) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey55", linewidth = 0.45) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(data = tidy_data |> filter(Year %% 5 == 0), size = 2.5, shape = 21, fill = "white", stroke = 1.1) +
  geom_text(data = labels_2025, aes(label = label), hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~Income_Group, ncol = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = col_measure, name = NULL) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("Real Income" = "solid", "HDB Resale Price" = "solid"), name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2025, by = 5), expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.14))) +
scale_y_continuous(
  breaks = seq(60, 180, by = 20),
  limits = c(55, 185)
) +
  labs(title = "Real Household Income vs HDB Resale Price Index (2010 = 100)",
       subtitle = "Singapore, 2000-2025  |  Income deflated by group-specific CPI  |  Shaded band = affordability gap",
       x = NULL, y = "Index value (2010 = 100)",
        caption = "Sources: SingStat Table Builder — Average Monthly Household Employment Income by Decile; SingStat — CPI by Household Income Group; HDB / data.gov.sg — HDB resale flat prices.") +
  base_theme + theme(legend.text = element_text(size = 13), legend.spacing.x = unit(0.4, "cm"))
```

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Nurul Zahirah.** This is our main improved visualisation. Each panel represents one income group, and within each panel we compare real income and HDB resale prices on the same indexed scale, with 2010 set to 100. Because both series now share one common axis, the comparison is visually honest. The shaded band highlights the affordability gap, and the endpoint labels show the exact 2025 values. The overall pattern is clear: the gap starts around 2012-13, then accelerates sharply post-2020, with housing prices rising faster than real income for all three groups.
:::


## 4.2 How to Read the Chart {style="font-size: 0.78em;"}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="58%"}

- Each panel = one **income group** (Bottom 20%, Middle 60%, Top 20%)
- [Blue line]{style="color: #2166AC; font-weight: bold;"} = **real income** (inflation-adjusted)
- [Red line]{style="color: #B2182B; font-weight: bold;"} = **HDB resale price** index
- Both indexed to **2010 = 100** — same scale, genuine comparison
- [Shaded band]{style="color: #B2182B;"} = **affordability gap**
- Grey bands = **GFC** (2008–09) and **COVID-19** (2020–21)
- **Bold numbers** at right = exact 2025 values

:::

::: {.column width="42%"}

### Why this redesign works
- **Honest comparison**  
  One shared indexed axis removes dual-axis distortion

- **Less clutter**  
  Three grouped panels replace ten overlapping lines

- **Faster interpretation**  
  Gap ribbon, event shading, and endpoint labels highlight the main message immediately

:::
::::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Nurul Zahirah.** This slide explains how to interpret the redesign. The most important feature is that both measures use the same indexed scale, so differences in slope and distance are meaningful. Faceting separates the three income groups cleanly, while the shaded ribbon turns affordability into a visible object rather than something the viewer has to estimate mentally. The event bands and endpoint labels then help the audience connect the pattern to major economic shocks and the latest 2025 values. I'll now hand over to Anushka to discuss the insights from the chart.
:::
# 5.0 Insights & Analysis


## 5.1 Key Insights {style="font-size: 0.78em;"}

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false

tribble(
  ~Period, ~Observation, ~Key_Figure,
  "**Before 2010**", "Real income broadly kept pace with HDB prices across all groups", "Affordability gap **negative** (income ahead)",
  "**Before 2010**", "GFC (2008–09) temporarily compressed the gap as housing dipped", "Housing prices dipped during crisis",
  "**2010 Onward**", "Resale prices diverge upward from income across all groups", "Gap widens **progressively**",
  "**2010 Onward**", "Acceleration after 2020: pent-up demand + construction delays + low rates", "Post-COVID surge drives gap",
  "**By 2025**", "Bottom 20%: income index 140.5 vs HDB 171.6", "Gap = **+31.1 pts** (most policy-relevant)",
  "**By 2025**", "Middle 60%: income index 141.8 vs HDB 171.6", "Gap = **+29.8 pts**",
  "**By 2025**", "Top 20%: income index 113.8 vs HDB 171.6", "Gap = **+57.8 pts** (see note below)"
) |>
  gt() |> fmt_markdown(columns = c(Period, Key_Figure)) |>
  cols_label(Period = "Period", Observation = "What We Observe", Key_Figure = "Key Figure") |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = 18, column_labels.background.color = "lightblue",
              column_labels.font.weight = "bold", table.width = pct(100), data_row.padding = px(5)) |>
  cols_width(Period ~ px(170), Observation ~ px(550), Key_Figure ~ px(380)) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_fill(color = "gray95"), locations = cells_body(rows = c(2, 4, 6))) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_fill(color = "#FFF3F3"), locations = cells_body(rows = 5))
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.75em; margin-top: 8px;"}
**Bottom 20% gap is most policy-relevant** — lower absolute income, least financial buffer, weakest 2020–2025 growth.

**Why the Top 20% gap appears largest:** The Top 20% have a higher CPI basket (more services, discretionary spending), so CPI deflation reduces their *real* income index more aggressively. This inflates the *index-based* gap but does not mean they face greater hardship — they still have the highest absolute income and strongest financial buffers.
:::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Anushka.** Let me highlight the key findings. Before 2010, income generally kept pace with housing. After 2010, prices diverged, and the gap accelerated sharply post-COVID. By 2025, the Bottom 20% face a 31-point gap — and that is the most policy-relevant figure because these households have the least financial buffer. You'll notice the Top 20% gap looks even larger at 58 points, but this is largely an artefact of CPI deflation: their higher CPI basket reduces the real income index more. In absolute dollar terms, they still earn the most.
:::


## 5.2 Affordability Gap Over Time

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5.5

gap_labels_2025 <- gap_data |> filter(Year == 2025) |>
  mutate(label = paste0(Income_Group, ": +", round(Affordability_Gap, 1), " pts"),
         y_pos = case_when(Income_Group == "Top 20%" ~ 59, Income_Group == "Bottom 20%" ~ 32,
                           Income_Group == "Middle 60%" ~ 26))

ggplot(gap_data, aes(x = Year, y = Affordability_Gap, color = Income_Group)) +
  geom_rect(data = events, aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            inherit.aes = FALSE, fill = "grey50", alpha = 0.10) +
  geom_text(data = events, aes(x = (xmin + xmax) / 2, y = -39, label = label),
            inherit.aes = FALSE, size = 3, color = "grey35", fontface = "italic", angle = 90) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_text(data = gap_labels_2025, aes(y = y_pos, label = label),
            x = 2026, hjust = 0, size = 3.2, fontface = "bold", show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_color_manual(values = col_group, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2025, by = 5), limits = c(1999, 2033), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0))) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-40, 80, by = 20), limits = c(-42, 85)) +
  labs(title = "Affordability Gap by Income Group",
       subtitle = "Gap = HDB index - Real income index  |  Positive = housing outpacing income",
       x = NULL, y = "Gap (index points)", caption = "Sources: SingStat Table Builder — Average Monthly Household Employment Income by Decile; SingStat — CPI by Household Income Group; HDB / data.gov.sg — HDB resale flat prices.") +
  base_theme
```

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Anushka.** This chart isolates the affordability gap itself — the difference between HDB price index and real income index. When the line is above zero, housing is outpacing income. You can see all three groups cross zero around 2012–2013, and then the gap accelerates dramatically after COVID-19. The endpoint labels confirm the exact 2025 gap for each group.
:::


## 5.3 Growth Rate Comparison (CAGR)

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 13
#| fig-height: 5.2

cagr_fn <- function(end_val, start_val, n) round(((end_val / start_val)^(1 / n) - 1) * 100, 2)
periods <- list(c(2000, 2010), c(2010, 2020), c(2020, 2025))

income_cagr <- map_dfr(periods, function(p) {
  wide <- tidy_data |> filter(Measure_Type == "Real Income", Year %in% p) |>
    pivot_wider(names_from = Year, values_from = Index_Value)
  wide |> mutate(CAGR = cagr_fn(!!sym(as.character(p[2])), !!sym(as.character(p[1])), p[2] - p[1]),
                 Sub_Period = paste0(p[1], " to ", p[2]), Measure = "Real Income") |>
    select(Income_Group, Sub_Period, Measure, CAGR)
})
hdb_cagr <- map_dfr(periods, function(p) {
  wide <- tidy_data |> filter(Measure_Type == "HDB Resale Price", Year %in% p) |>
    distinct(Year, Index_Value) |> pivot_wider(names_from = Year, values_from = Index_Value)
  tibble(CAGR = cagr_fn(wide[[as.character(p[2])]], wide[[as.character(p[1])]], p[2] - p[1]),
         Sub_Period = paste0(p[1], " to ", p[2]), Measure = "HDB Resale Price")
}) |> crossing(Income_Group = c("Bottom 20%", "Middle 60%", "Top 20%"))

cagr_data <- bind_rows(income_cagr, hdb_cagr) |>
  mutate(Income_Group = factor(Income_Group, levels = c("Bottom 20%", "Middle 60%", "Top 20%")),
         Sub_Period = factor(Sub_Period, levels = c("2000 to 2010", "2010 to 2020", "2020 to 2025")),
         Measure = factor(Measure, levels = c("Real Income", "HDB Resale Price")))

ggplot(cagr_data, aes(x = Sub_Period, y = CAGR, fill = Measure)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(0.65), width = 0.55) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.4, color = "grey40") +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(CAGR, 1), "%"), vjust = ifelse(CAGR >= 0, -0.4, 1.3)),
            position = position_dodge(0.65), size = 3.2, fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~Income_Group, ncol = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = col_measure, name = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"), breaks = seq(-2, 10, by = 2)) +
  labs(title = "CAGR of Real Income vs HDB Resale Prices by Sub-Period",
       subtitle = "Three periods: 2000-2010, 2010-2020, 2020-2025",
       x = NULL, y = "CAGR (%)", caption = "Sources: SingStat Table Builder — Average Monthly Household Employment Income by Decile; SingStat — CPI by Household Income Group; HDB / data.gov.sg — HDB resale flat prices.") +
  base_theme + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5))
```

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Anushka.** To quantify the divergence, we computed compound annual growth rates for three sub-periods: 2000 to 2010, 2010 to 2020, and 2020 to 2025. The clearest result is the post-2020 period. HDB resale prices grew at about 8.1 percent per year, while real income growth was much weaker across all groups, and even negative for the Top 20% after CPI deflation. This confirms that the post-COVID period is the main driver of the affordability gap we saw in the earlier charts.
:::

## 5.4 Analysis Commentary {style="font-size: 0.80em;"}

<hr>

### 2000–2010
- HDB prices already grew faster than income in some years
- Early warning signs of affordability stress were visible
- Gap was present, but still relatively moderate

### 2010–2020
- Cooling measures helped slow resale price growth
- Income growth recovered somewhat
- The gap stabilised, but did not close

### 2020–2025
- HDB resale prices surged most sharply in the whole period
- Construction delays, low interest rates, and pent-up demand likely contributed
- This period is the clearest inflection point in worsening affordability

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Anushka.** This slide summarises the story across the three periods. From 2000 to 2010, there were already early warning signs, but the affordability gap remained relatively moderate. Between 2010 and 2020, cooling measures helped slow resale price growth and the gap stabilised, though it never fully closed. The clearest inflection point is 2020 to 2025, when HDB resale prices surged much faster than real income across all groups. I'll now hand back to Shina for the conclusion.
:::

# 6.0 Conclusion


## 6.1 Summary & Takeaways {style="font-size: 0.75em;"}

<hr>

**Did income keep pace with housing prices?**

```{r}
#| echo: false

tribble(
  ~Period, ~Answer, ~Detail,
  "**Before 2010**", "Broadly **yes**", "Real income kept pace across all income groups",
  "**After 2010**", "**No**", "Gap widened progressively, accelerated sharply since 2020",
  "**Bottom 20%**", "Most acute **pressure**", "Bottom 20% face a 31-point gap — 140.5 vs 171.6 Weakest income growth, least financial buffer, largest expenditure share on housing"
) |>
  gt() |> fmt_markdown(columns = c(Period, Answer)) |>
  cols_label(Period = "Period", Answer = "Answer", Detail = "Detail") |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = 19, column_labels.background.color = "lightblue",
              column_labels.font.weight = "bold", table.width = pct(100), data_row.padding = px(7)) |>
  cols_width(Period ~ px(170), Answer ~ px(200), Detail ~ px(700)) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_fill(color = "#FFF3F3"), locations = cells_body(rows = 3))
```

<br>

**What we improved**

```{r}
#| echo: false

tribble(
  ~Original_Problem, ~Our_Solution,
  "Dual-axis distortion", "**Common indexed scale** (2010 = 100) — single y-axis",
  "10 congested decile lines", "**3 meaningful groups** — Bottom 20%, Middle 60%, Top 20%",
  "Nominal (unadjusted) income", "**Group-specific CPI** deflation for honest comparison",
  "No visible affordability metric", "**Shaded ribbon** + endpoint labels + event annotations"
) |>
  gt() |> fmt_markdown(columns = Our_Solution) |>
  cols_label(Original_Problem = "Original Problem", Our_Solution = "Our Solution") |>
  tab_options(table.font.size = 18, column_labels.background.color = "lightblue",
              column_labels.font.weight = "bold", table.width = pct(100), data_row.padding = px(6)) |>
  cols_width(Original_Problem ~ px(350), Our_Solution ~ px(700)) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_fill(color = "gray95"), locations = cells_body(rows = c(2, 4)))
```

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Shina.** To conclude, our answer is mixed. Before 2010, real income broadly kept pace with housing prices. After 2010, and especially after 2020, HDB resale prices rose faster than real income and the affordability gap widened. This pressure is most policy-relevant for the Bottom 20%, because they have the least financial buffer. In improving the original chart, we replaced a potentially misleading comparison with a cleaner and more defensible one: a common indexed scale, three meaningful income groups, group-specific CPI deflation, and a directly visible affordability gap.
:::


## 6.2 Limitations & Future Work {style="font-size: 0.82em;"}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Limitations

- **Median annual resale price** — no flat type/location variation
- **Employment income only** — excludes transfers, CPF top-ups
- **CPI deflation** — no mortgage rate / debt servicing costs
- **Index rebasing** — obscures absolute level differences

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Possible Extensions

- Break down by **flat type** (3-room, 4-room, 5-room)
- Incorporate **government grants** (CPF Housing Grant, AHCG)
- Add **geographic dimension** (town-level prices)
- Include **mortgage servicing ratios**

:::
::::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Shina.** Finally, we want to be transparent about the scope of our analysis. We used median annual resale prices, so we do not capture differences by flat type or town. Our income measure is employment income only, which excludes transfers and grants, and CPI deflation does not directly account for mortgage servicing costs. These are useful directions for future work, alongside adding flat-type, geographic, and grant-level detail for a fuller picture of affordability.
:::

## Thank You {.unnumbered .unlisted style="text-align: center;"}

<br><br>

### Real Income vs Housing Prices in Singapore {style="color: #2166AC;"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em; margin-top: 8px;"}
Did income growth keep pace with HDB resale prices, 2000–2025?
:::

<br>

Shina · Jeanie · Claudia · Nurul Zahirah · Anushka

<br>

AAI1001 – Data Engineering and Visualization | AY25/26 Tri 2

<br>

::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: grey;"}
Questions?
:::

::: {.notes}
**Speaker: Shina.** Thank you for watching our presentation. We hope our redesign made the affordability story clearer and more evidence-based. We welcome any questions.
:::
